chapters.title: Pre-Mathematical Concepts
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify basic comparative and positional concepts such as fat/thin, big/small, inside/outside, above/below and more/less.

chapters.title: Shapes
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify common two-dimensional and three-dimensional shapes in everyday objects
Students will be able to match objects that share the same geometric shape
Students will be able to draw objects that are larger or smaller than a given model
activity_summary: Observe pictures and name objects that resemble a ball, top or brick (pdf 16).
Match pictures of objects to shapes and to each other based on geometric similarity (pdf 17–19).
Draw objects that are bigger, smaller, thinner or more/less in number than the examples shown (pdf 14–15, 20).

chapters.title: Numbers from 1 to 5
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to count objects and match groups up to 5

chapters.title: Numbers from 6 to 9
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to count objects and identify numbers from 6 to 9
Students will be able to match sets with equal numbers and recognise differences in quantities
Students will be able to write the numerals 6, 7, 8 and 9
activity_summary: Counting objects in pictures to identify numbers 6–9
Matching pictures to corresponding dot patterns and identical pictures
Tracing and writing numerals 6, 7, 8 and 9
Exercise activities asking pupils to spot differing sets and draw required counts

chapters.title: Before-After-Between-More-Less
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to write and recognise numbers from 1 to 9
Students will be able to identify the number before, after and between given numbers
Students will be able to compare two groups to decide more, less or equal and order numbers ascending/descending

chapters.title: Zero (0)
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify zero (0) as representing none.
activity_summary: Sing the parrot song with actions to introduce the idea that 'none remained' and label it as zero.

chapters.title: Addition of Numbers Total not Exceeding 9
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to add two small numbers where the total does not exceed 9
Students will be able to recognise and write zero
curriculum_tags: Addition of Numbers Total not Exceeding 9
activity_summary: Counting beads and writing the number to introduce zero (pages 64–65).
Adding one to a small group by visual counting of beads (page 66).
Adding two small groups (horizontal and vertical), drawing dots, and computing totals up to 9 (pages 67–70).
Exercises on adding zero, matching sums with pictures, and practice problems (pages 71–72).

chapters.title: Subtraction of Numbers upto 9
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify differences using subtraction with pictures
Students will be able to subtract single-digit numbers and understand the subtraction symbol
Students will be able to apply subtraction facts in written exercises up to 9

chapters.title: Numbers from 10 to 20
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify bundles of ten in pictures
Students will be able to read and write numbers from 10 to 20 and represent them as tens and ones
Students will be able to order numbers from 10 to 20 and find the numbers before, after and between given numbers

chapters.title: Addition of Numbers, the Total not Exceeding 20
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to add two numbers whose total does not exceed 20
curriculum_tags: Addition of Numbers, the Total not Exceeding 20
activity_summary: Use bundles of ten sticks and loose sticks to represent sums under 20 and write the numbers in blank boxes.
Practice adding pairs of numbers horizontally as shown in the worked example.

chapters.title: Digital Literacy
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify what a computer is
Students will be able to name and recognise basic parts of a computer
Students will be able to describe ways computers help with learning, playing and exploring
activity_summary: A matching activity where pupils draw lines to match listed computer activities (writing, drawing, watching, playing) with pictures.

chapters.title: Subtraction of Numbers not Exceeding 20
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to subtract a single-digit number from a two-digit number
Students will be able to count remaining objects after subtraction and record the result correctly
activity_summary: Visual activity using pictures (bottles and other objects) to illustrate subtraction within 20.
Guided counting of remaining objects after items are taken away; use of bundles and loose sticks suggested as manipulatives.

chapters.title: Introduction of Tens from 10 to 100
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to count in tens from 10 to 100 and recognise the tens names (ten, twenty, thirty, …, hundred)
activity_summary: Counting bundles of sticks in groups of ten to build familiarity with the sequence 10, 20, 30, …, 100

chapters.title: Numbers from 20 to 100
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to write numbers from 20 to 100 as sums of tens and ones
activity_summary: Counting bundles of sticks and loose sticks to form numbers and express them as tens + ones across ranges 21–100.
Completing a 1–100 number grid by filling missing numbers.

chapters.title: Numbers Before, Between, After
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to count groups of objects and match them to the correct numerals
Students will be able to identify the number before, after and between given numbers and describe simple positional questions on a seating chart
Students will be able to compare two or more numbers to find the smaller or larger and write numbers in words up to 20

chapters.title: Money
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify the value of common coins and currency notes
Students will be able to choose and write the value of given coins and notes

concept_nodes.label: NUMBER_SENSE
concept_nodes.description: Understanding basic comparative, quantitative and positional vocabulary and concepts that prepare children for formal number learning.

concept_relationships.note: Early comparative and spatial language supports later arithmetic problem contexts.

concept_nodes.label: COMPARISON
concept_nodes.description: Concepts that express more/less, bigger/smaller, thicker/thinner and counting-related comparisons.

concept_nodes.label: SPATIAL_RELATIONS
concept_nodes.description: Positional vocabulary such as above/below, inside/outside, under, deeper that describe location relations between objects.

concept_nodes.label: GEOMETRY
concept_nodes.description: Study of shapes, their properties and spatial relations, including 2D and 3D forms.

concept_relationships.note: Understanding shapes supports spatial reasoning tasks.

concept_nodes.label: 2D Shapes
concept_nodes.description: Flat shapes such as circles, squares, rectangles, and triangles.

concept_relationships.note: Recognising 2D shapes is part of shape recognition skills.

concept_nodes.label: 3D Shapes
concept_nodes.description: Solid shapes such as spheres, cubes, cylinders and cones.

concept_relationships.note: Recognising 3D shapes helps link objects to their geometric forms.

concept_nodes.label: SHAPE_RECOGNITION
concept_nodes.description: The skill of identifying and matching objects to geometric shapes.

concept_relationships.note: Visual pattern detection supports matching shapes.

concept_nodes.label: SPATIAL_REASONING
concept_nodes.description: Understanding positions, relationships and transformations in space.

concept_relationships.note: Spatial reasoning and geometry reinforce each other for object manipulation.

concept_nodes.label: ARITHMETIC
concept_nodes.description: Basic operations and manipulation of numbers (addition, subtraction) used later in computation.

concept_relationships.note: Children need number sense before learning formal arithmetic operations.

concept_nodes.label: PLACE_VALUE
concept_nodes.description: Understanding positions of digits and their values (ones, tens, etc.).

concept_relationships.note: Place value builds on basic number recognition.

concept_nodes.label: ADDITION
concept_nodes.description: Combining quantities to form a larger quantity.

concept_nodes.label: SUBTRACTION
concept_nodes.description: Removing quantities to form a smaller quantity.

concept_nodes.label: ZERO
concept_nodes.description: The concept and symbol representing 'none' or no quantity.

concept_relationships.note: Understanding zero is part of number sense

concept_nodes.label: NUMBER_SEQUENCE
concept_nodes.description: Ordering numbers and identifying predecessor, successor and numbers between.

concept_relationships.note: Recognising tens and ones aids in sequencing numbers from 10 to 20.

concept_nodes.label: COMPUTATIONAL_THINKING
concept_nodes.description: Top-level concept covering basic understanding of computers, their parts, and common uses in learning and play.

concept_relationships.note: Foundational numeracy supports using some educational software and understanding digital representations.

concept_nodes.label: COMPUTER_PARTS
concept_nodes.description: Concept node for identifying and naming physical components such as monitor, CPU, keyboard and mouse.

concept_relationships.note: Recognising parts is a subskill of basic computational understanding.

concept_nodes.label: USES_OF_COMPUTERS
concept_nodes.description: Concept node for common computer activities: writing, drawing, watching educational content and playing games.

concept_relationships.note: Understanding uses builds on basic computer familiarity.

concept_nodes.label: CURRENCY_RECOGNITION
concept_nodes.description: Recognising coins and notes and understanding their face values.

concept_relationships.note: Basic number sense is needed to name and compare coin/note values.

topics.name: Recognising comparative and positional concepts from pictures
topics.summary: Learners observe a classroom picture and practise using words that describe size, shape and position (for example fat/thin, big/small, inside/outside and above/below). The teacher-led prompts develop vocabulary and visual observation skills needed before formal numeracy.
topics.embedding_text: Grade 1 Mathematics: Pre-Mathematical Concepts — Observing pictures to identify comparative (more/less, big/small) and positional (above/below, inside/outside) words that prepare students for number concepts.
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to identify and use basic comparative and positional vocabulary (fat/thin, big/small, inside/outside, above/below, more/less).

outcome_evidence.outcome: Students will be able to identify and use basic comparative and positional vocabulary (fat/thin, big/small, inside/outside, above/below, more/less).
outcome_evidence.evidence_text: Get your pupils to observe the above picture. Help them to understand the basic concepts of fat - thin, big - small, inside - outside, above - below, more - less etc.

common_misconceptions: Children may confuse size adjectives (big/small) with distance (near/far).
Children may interpret 'deeper' as 'bigger' rather than positional depth.
mastery_signals: Can point out and name objects using comparative words correctly when asked.
Responds consistently to 'which is more/less' questions about pictured groups.
assessment_indicators: Questions asking 'Which flower pot has more flowers?' or 'Which tree looks bigger?'

retrieval_anchors.text_span: Get your pupils to observe the above picture. Help them to understand the basic concepts of fat - thin, big - small, inside - outside, above - below, more - less etc.
Look at the picture. Answer the questions orally.

visual_assets.spatial_description: A single composite illustration showing multiple objects at different positions and sizes (animals, people, plants, objects) used for oral question prompts.
visual_assets.pedagogical_purpose: Provides a rich visual context for asking positional and comparative questions to build observation and vocabulary.

topics.summary: Structured practice items where learners mark answers about position and comparative quantity (for example which object is under the table, which group has less chairs). These exercises check observational understanding and correct use of comparative and positional terms.

outcome_evidence.outcome: Students will be able to select or mark the object that matches positional or comparative descriptions in a picture.
outcome_evidence.evidence_text: Indicate your answer by '✓' in the □ .

common_misconceptions: Marking the visually larger object as 'deeper' or misunderstanding 'under' vs 'inside'.
mastery_signals: Correctly marks the box for positional questions such as 'Which object is under the table?'
Chooses the group with fewer items when asked 'Which group has less chairs?'
